title: Te Whakamahi Hangarau mō te Whakahaumanu o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Technology for the Restoration of Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Technology
culturalContext: Students will explore how technology can support the restoration of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, a culturally significant stream that flows through our rohe. This awa is mentioned in Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau and was traditionally a source of mahinga kai. Students will connect with local kaumātua to understand the historical significance and current challenges facing the awa, using this knowledge to inform technological solutions.
learningObjectives: Design and create a digital model or prototype that addresses a specific environmental challenge facing Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, incorporating both technological principles and mātauranga Māori.
Collaborate effectively with peers and community experts to gather and integrate cultural and environmental knowledge into technological practice.
Critically evaluate the potential impacts (both positive and negative) of a proposed technological solution on the awa and its surrounding ecosystem.

activities.title: He Haerenga: Awa Immersion
activities.description: Visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with a local kaumātua or environmental ranger. Listen to pūrākau (stories) of the awa, observe its current state, and use water testing kits and tablets to photograph and document specific environmental issues (e.g., clarity, potential pollutants, bank erosion).

activities.title: Te Whakawhiti Kōrero: Interviewing Knowledge Holders
activities.description: In small groups, prepare for and conduct an interview (in person or via video call) with a kaumātua or a representative from a local environmental group like Nga Herenga. Develop questions to understand what a 'healthy awa' means from different cultural and scientific perspectives.

activities.title: Te Hōtaka: Prototype Design & Development
activities.description: Using the data and knowledge gathered, groups will choose a specific issue to address (e.g., monitoring water quality, deterring pest fish, promoting native planting). They will use Tinkercad or a similar platform to design a 3D model of a tool or device, or develop a plan for a simple app or sensor system that could help solve the problem.

activities.title: Te Whakaaturanga: Gallery Walk & Feedback
activities.description: Groups present their digital prototypes or design plans in a classroom 'gallery'. Peers and the teacher provide structured feedback based on criteria of cultural appropriateness, feasibility, and potential environmental impact, using a 'I like...', 'I wonder...', 'What if...' protocol.

resources: Kaumātua from local Ngāti Kahungunu marae (e.g., representatives from Nga Herenga environmental trust)
Tinkercad or other free 3D modelling software
Digital tablets with cameras and water testing kits (e.g., pH strips, turbidity tubes)
'Te Mātauranga o Ngāti Kahungunu' resources from the Pātaka Mātauranga o Kahungunu website

tasks: Submit a digital portfolio containing: interview notes, photographs/water test data, and the link to their Tinkercad prototype or app design plan.
Deliver a 3-minute group presentation justifying their design choices, explaining how it integrates mātauranga Māori and technology, and evaluates its potential impact.

nzcAlignment: Technology: Technological Practice (Level 4 - brief development, planning for practice, outcome development and evaluation)
Technology: Nature of Technology (Level 4 - characteristics of technological outcomes)
Social Sciences: Place and Environment (Level 4 - understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges)
The Principles: Treaty of Waitangi, Cultural Diversity, Community Engagement
